text: The surgeon lifts the sigmoid colon to identify the inferior mesenteric artery, making a division of the peritoneum at the sacral promontory. The left ureter and autonomic nerves are visible. The inferior mesenteric artery is clipped and divided near its root, while the autonomic nerves are carefully pushed down to safeguard them.
The surgeon pushes down the gonadal vessels and lifts the descending colon towards the lateral abdominal wall. The inferior mesenteric vein is divided at a high point.

The surgeon drops the gonadal vein and divides the mesocolon of the transverse colon, revealing the pancreatic body and tail. The dissection continues along the pancreatic tail, lifting the mesocolon of the distal transverse colon until the spleen is visible. The splenic flexure of the colon is lifted, ensuring the dissection remains above the upper pole of the kidney and the perinephric fat.

The surgeon continues to divide the transverse mesocolon medially, opening the lesser sac and revealing the stomach. The distal transverse colon is fully mobilized off the retroperitoneum, providing clear visualization of the stomach.

The surgeon returns to the pelvic side, identifying the left autonomic nerve while lifting the rectum. The right autonomic nerve is also visible. The dissection remains anterior and medial to the autonomic nerves, ensuring the ureters are safeguarded. Minimal bleeding is observed as the surgeon uses either a monopolar or harmonic energy source to maintain the proper surgical plane.
The surgeon dissects into the pre-circular hollow of a female patient, navigating through loose tissue areas while remaining medial to the right autonomic nerve bundle.

The surgeon uses a vascular ligating instrument, such as a harmonic or ligature, to address vessels encountered while dissecting lower in the surgical field. The complete mesocolic and mesorectal tissues are visibly removed.
The surgeon continues dissection along the mesorectal plane, ensuring no violation of the plane, until reaching the pelvic floor where the muscles are visible and clean of surrounding tissues.

The surgeon proceeds with dissection on the left side, identifying the left ureter and dividing the peritoneum medial to the ovarian pedicle. The ovarian pedicle is pushed laterally, safeguarding the autonomic nerve bundles. The lateral support of the rectum is divided to encompass lymphatic tissues, with dissection extending laterally below the level of the main nerve bundles along the lateral pelvic wall.

The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator or ethylene suture to lift the uterus in a female patient. They proceed to divide the tissue between the posterior vaginal wall and the anterior wall of the rectum, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon continues dissection in the proper anatomical plane, moving laterally to clear mesorectal tissues and remove fibrofatty tissues, ensuring no lymphatic tissues remain.

The surgeon identifies the level for rectal dissection and thins out the rectal tube by dividing lateral fatty tissues to minimize staple usage. The rectum is divided at a low level, confirmed to be beyond the small tumor by palpation, using a single firing of a green or gold cartridge.
In a narrow male android pelvis, the surgeon uses multiple firings to divide the low rectum. The left lateral peritoneal attachment of the descending colon is dissected using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon divides the distal half of the gastrocolic ligament, having already freed the mesocolon from the retroperitoneal area. The gastrocolic ligament is divided on the left side, facilitating the movement of the colon. A small suprapubic incision is made to bring out the specimen, which is then inserted into the colon for anastomosis. The remaining colon is visibly maneuvered into the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon confirms the completion of the anastomotic line and performs a leak test to ensure there is no leakage, indicating that a colostomy is not necessary.
